persona.name: Ravi Mehta

occupation.title: Senior Data Scientist
occupation.description: Builds predictive models for cancer treatment outcomes using genomic data. Works hybrid — 3 days in the office, 2 remote. Spends most of his time in Jupyter notebooks and on Slack. High cognitive demand, sedentary.

persona.education: B.Tech in Computer Science from IIT Bombay. M.S. in Statistics from Stanford. Published two papers on causal inference methods.
long_term_goals: Transition to a VP of Data Science role
Optimize his running using the same analytical rigor he applies to work
Build a personalized training model using his own biometric data
Visit every Michelin-starred restaurant in the Bay Area
persona.style: Ravi speaks in data. He quantifies everything — not to show off, but because precision is how he makes sense of the world. He asks for energy return percentages, stack height in millimeters, and weight in grams — marketing adjectives like 'responsive' annoy him unless backed by numbers. He's quiet in group settings but sharp in one-on-one conversation. Dresses in fitted running gear during runs and tech-casual (Patagonia vest, Allbirds) at work.

traits: Analytically rigorous — won't accept claims without evidence
Introverted but socially capable in small groups
Perfectionistic about running form and metrics
Impatient with vague or qualitative product descriptions
Enjoys the intellectual side of running optimization
Spends as much time analyzing runs as running them

likes: Shoes with published midsole energy return data
Brands that provide detailed specifications (not just marketing copy)
Carbon plate or similar propulsion technology
Shoes with large-sample runner reviews (not just influencer opinions)
A/B testing his own shoe rotation
dislikes: Marketing copy that says 'responsive' without defining the metric
Shoes that don't publish their stack height or heel-toe drop
Overly cushioned shoes that sacrifice proprioception
Products positioned on emotion rather than evidence
Running shoe reviews that don't mention weight or durability data
Being told to 'just run in whatever feels good'

beliefs: Running performance is a multivariate optimization problem
Shoe choice should be data-informed, not brand-loyalty-driven
The best running shoe is the one with the highest evidence base for your gait type
Technology in shoes should be measurable, not just marketable
skills: Statistical modeling and machine learning (Python, R)
Running gait self-analysis using slow-motion video

general: Runs 4x per week with every run tracked on Garmin + Stryd pod
Maintains a personal spreadsheet comparing shoes by weight, drop, stack height, and durability
Reads Doctors of Running and Believe in the Run for technical reviews
Rotates 4 pairs of shoes: easy day, tempo, long run, race

morning: Review yesterday's running data on Garmin Connect
Run if it's a morning-run day (Tu, Th)
workday: Lunch: bento from Marufuku or homemade dal
evening: Run if it's an evening-run day (M, F)
Cook dinner — usually Indian or Japanese
Analyze run data and update shoe log
Read research papers or running blogs
weekend: Saturday: long run (12-16 miles) through Golden Gate Park

health.physical: Good cardiovascular fitness but tight hip flexors from desk work. Minor ITBS flare-up in 2025 resolved with targeted strengthening. Neutral gait with slight overpronation under fatigue. Maintains healthy BMI and tracks body composition monthly.
health.mental: Manages work stress well. Occasionally overthinks running — analysis paralysis about shoe choice and training plans. Meditation practice helps. Gets satisfaction from seeing training data trend in the right direction.

relationships.name: Anita Mehta
relationships.description: Lives in Mumbai. Doesn't understand why he runs but is proud of his career. Video calls every Sunday.

relationships.name: Nisha Patel
relationships.description: Product manager at Stripe. Not a runner but hikes. Tolerates his running data spreadsheets with affectionate exasperation.

relationships.name: Arun Krishnamurthy
relationships.description: Also a data scientist. They discuss shoe technology on long runs. Arun is faster and Ravi uses him as a pace benchmark.

relationships.name: Raj Mehta
relationships.description: Retired banker in Mumbai. Had knee replacement surgery — Ravi thinks about this when choosing shoes.
